q:
A thin tube sealed at both ends is $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ long. It lies horizontally, the middle $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ containing mercury and two equal ends containing air at standard atmospheric pressure. If the tube is now turned to a vertical position, by what amount will the mercury be displaced ?
(Given : cross-section of the tube can be assumed to be uniform)

subject: Physics
q: A soap bubble of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is formed inside another soap bubble of radius, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The radius of an equivalent soap bubble that has the same excess pressure as inside the smaller bubble with respect to the atmospheric pressure is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A soap bubble of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is formed inside another soap bubble of radius, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The radius of an equivalent soap bubble that has the same excess pressure as inside the smaller bubble with respect to the atmospheric pressure is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
The formula of excess pressure inside the soap bubble is, 4TR here T is the surface tension and R is the radius of the soap bubble, 

⇒P0+4T6+4T3=P0+4TR

⇒16+13=1R⇒R=2 cm.

subject: Physics
q: Water flows through a horizontal tube as shown in the figure. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at $A$ and $B$ are $6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ respectively. The rate of flow will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$. (take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question: Water flows through a horizontal tube as shown in the figure. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at $A$ and $B$ are $6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ respectively. The rate of flow will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$. (take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
Given: $h = 5$ cm, $A_1 = 6$ cm², $A_2 = 3$ cm², $g = 1000$ cm/s²
By continuity: $A_1v_1 = A_2v_2 \Rightarrow v_2 = 2v_1$
By Bernoulli's equation: $P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$
Since $P_1 - P_2 = \rho gh$:
$\rho gh = \frac{1}{2}\rho(4v_1^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{3\rho v_1^2}{2}$
$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 5}{3}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}$ cm/s
Rate of flow $Q = A_1v_1 = 6 \times \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} = 200\sqrt{3}$ cm³/s

subject: Physics
q: A water drop of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> equal droplets. The surface tension of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>075</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. In this process the gain in surface energy will be
question: A water drop of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> equal droplets. The surface tension of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>075</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. In this process the gain in surface energy will be
solution:
By conserving the total volume 43πR3=64×43πr3⇒R=4r

Change in surface energy is

ΔU=T×ΔA, =T64×4πr2-4πR2

=4πTR264×116-1=12×3.14×0.075×10-4

≈2.8×10-4 J

subject: Physics
q: A square hole of side length $\ell$ is made at a depth of $\mathrm{h}$ and a circular hole of radius $\mathrm{r}$ is made at a depth of $4 \mathrm{~h}$ from the surface of water in a water tank kept on a horizontal surface. If $\ell< < h, r< < h$ and the rate of water flow from the holes is the same, then $r$ is equal to
question: A square hole of side length $\ell$ is made at a depth of $\mathrm{h}$ and a circular hole of radius $\mathrm{r}$ is made at a depth of $4 \mathrm{~h}$ from the surface of water in a water tank kept on a horizontal surface. If $\ell< < h, r< < h$ and the rate of water flow from the holes is the same, then $r$ is equal to
solution:
As $A_1 v_1=A_2 v_2$ (Principle of continuity) or, $\ell^2 \sqrt{2 g h}=\pi r^2 \sqrt{2 g \times 4 h}$
(Efflux velocity $=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}})$
$\therefore \quad r^2=\frac{\ell^2}{2 \pi} \quad$ or $\quad r=\sqrt{\frac{\ell^2}{2 \pi}}=\frac{\ell}{\sqrt{2 \pi}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a cylindrical tank of radius 1 m is filled with water. The top surface of water is at 15 m from the bottom of the cylinder. There is a hole on the wall of cylinder at a height of 5 m from the bottom. A force of 5×105 N is applied an the top surface of water using a piston. The speed of efflux from the hole will be :
(given atmospheric pressure PA=1.01×105 Pa, density of water ρw=1000 kg m-3 and gravitational acceleration g=10 m s-2)
question:
Consider a cylindrical tank of radius 1 m is filled with water. The top surface of water is at 15 m from the bottom of the cylinder. There is a hole on the wall of cylinder at a height of 5 m from the bottom. A force of 5×105 N is applied an the top surface of water using a piston. The speed of efflux from the hole will be :
(given atmospheric pressure PA=1.01×105 Pa, density of water ρw=1000 kg m-3 and gravitational acceleration g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Assuming there is no atmospheric pressure on piston, P1=FπR2 and P2=P0.

Applying Bernoulli's equation at point 1 and 2,

p1+ρgh1+12ρv12=p2+ρgh2+12ρv22

⇒FπR2+ρgh1-h2=P0+12ρv22

⇒v2=2FπR2ρ-2P0ρ+2gh1-h2

v2=2×5×1053.14×12×1000-2×1.01×1051000+2×10×10

≈17.8 m s-1

It is observed that the two tangents drawn from observed that the two tangents drawn from liquid-glass interfaces (from opp. sides of the capillary) make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>º</mo></math> with one another. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> is close to (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A capillary tube made of glass of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is dipped vertically in a beaker filled with methylene iodide (surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>667</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>) which rises to height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> in the tube. It is observed that the two tangents drawn from observed that the two tangents drawn from liquid-glass interfaces (from opp. sides of the capillary) make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>º</mo></math> with one another. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> is close to (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
h=2Tcosθρgr  θ=30°  P=667 kg m-3; T=1/20 N m-1; r=0.15×10-3

=2×120×32667×10×0.15×10-3=0.087m

subject: Physics
q: Water flows in a horizontal pipe whose one end is closed with a valve. The reading of the pressure gauge attached to the pipe is $P_1$. The reading of the pressure gauge falls to $P_2$ when the valve is opened. The speed of water flowing in the pipe is proportional to
question: Water flows in a horizontal pipe whose one end is closed with a valve. The reading of the pressure gauge attached to the pipe is $P_1$. The reading of the pressure gauge falls to $P_2$ when the valve is opened. The speed of water flowing in the pipe is proportional to
solution: Using Bernoulli's theorem $\begin{aligned} & P_1-P_2=\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 \\ & v \propto \sqrt{P_1-P_2} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The pressure acting on a submarine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math> at a certain depth. If the depth is doubled, the percentage increase in the pressure acting on the submarine would be: (Assume that atmospheric pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math> density of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The pressure acting on a submarine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math> at a certain depth. If the depth is doubled, the percentage increase in the pressure acting on the submarine would be: (Assume that atmospheric pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math> density of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
P1=ρgd+P0=3×105 Pa

∴ ρgd=2×105 Pa

P2=2ρgd+P0

=4×105+105=5×105 Pa

% increase =P2-P1P1×100

=5×105-3×1053×105×100=2003%

q: Suppose you have taken a dilute solution of oleic acid in such a way that its concentration becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of oleic acid per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of the solution. Then you make a thin film of this solution (monomolecular thickness) of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> by considering <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> spherical drops of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mrow><mn>40</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the thickness of oleic acid layer will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________ .
question: Suppose you have taken a dilute solution of oleic acid in such a way that its concentration becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of oleic acid per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of the solution. Then you make a thin film of this solution (monomolecular thickness) of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> by considering <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> spherical drops of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mrow><mn>40</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the thickness of oleic acid layer will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________ .
solution:
4tT=100×43πr3

=100×4π3×340π×10-9=10-8 cm3

tT=25×10-10 cm=25×10-12 m

t0=0.01tT=25×10-14 m⇒x=25.

subject: Physics
q: A soap bubble is blown to a diameter of $7 \mathrm{~cm} .36960 \mathrm{erg}$ of work is done in blowing it further. If surface tension of soap solution is $40 \mathrm{dyne} / \mathrm{cm}$ then the new radius is______ $\mathrm{cm}$ Take $\left(\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
question: A soap bubble is blown to a diameter of $7 \mathrm{~cm} .36960 \mathrm{erg}$ of work is done in blowing it further. If surface tension of soap solution is $40 \mathrm{dyne} / \mathrm{cm}$ then the new radius is______ $\mathrm{cm}$ Take $\left(\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{S} \Delta \mathrm{A} \\ & 36960 \mathrm{erg}=\frac{40 \text { dyne }}{\mathrm{cm}} 8 \pi\left[(\mathrm{r})^2-\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^2\right] \mathrm{cm}^2 \\ & \mathrm{r}=7 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The reading of pressure metre attached with a closed pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. On opening the valve, water starts flowing and the reading of pressure metre falls to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of water is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>V</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is _________.
question: The reading of pressure metre attached with a closed pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. On opening the valve, water starts flowing and the reading of pressure metre falls to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of water is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>V</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is _________.
solution:
The formula to calculate the change in pressure can be written as

P1-P2=12ρv2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

4.5×104-2.0×104=12×103×v2⇒2.5×104=12×103×v2⇒v2=50⇒v=50

Hence, V=50.

subject: Physics
q: The average mass of rain drops is $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~kg}$ and their avarage terminal velocity is $9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Calculate the energy transferred by rain to each square metre of the surface at a place which receives $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ of rain in a year.
question: The average mass of rain drops is $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~kg}$ and their avarage terminal velocity is $9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Calculate the energy transferred by rain to each square metre of the surface at a place which receives $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ of rain in a year.
solution:
Total volume of rain drops, received $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ in a year by area $1 \mathrm{~m}^2$
$$
=1 \mathrm{~m}^2 \times \frac{100}{100} \mathrm{~m}=1 \mathrm{~m}^3
$$
As we know, density of water,
$$
d=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3
$$
Therefore, mass of this volume of water $M=d \times v=10^3 \times 1=10^3 \mathrm{~kg}$
Average terminal velocity of rain drop $v=9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ (given)
Therefore, energy transferred by rain,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
&=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 \times(9)^2 \\
&=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 \times 81=4.05 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> flows smoothly through a horizontal pipe that tapers in cross-sectional area from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The pressure difference between the wide and narrow sections of the pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. The rate of flow of liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> flows smoothly through a horizontal pipe that tapers in cross-sectional area from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The pressure difference between the wide and narrow sections of the pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. The rate of flow of liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
From the equation of continuity, v2=2v1.

Applying the Bernoulli's Theorem,

P1+ρv122=P2+ρ4v122  ⇒P1-P2=3ρv122

⇒4500=3×750×v122  ⇒v1=2 m s-1

Now volume flow rate,Q=Av1=1.2×10-2×2=24×10-3 m3 s-1

subject: Physics
q: If it takes 5 minutes to fill a 15 litre bucket from a water tap of diameter <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mi>π</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then the Reynolds number for the flow is (density of water = <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>  </mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi></mrow></mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mi> </mi><mi>v</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>y</mi><mi> </mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mi> </mi><mi>w</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>s</mi></math> ) close to:
question: If it takes 5 minutes to fill a 15 litre bucket from a water tap of diameter <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mi>π</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then the Reynolds number for the flow is (density of water = <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>  </mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi></mrow></mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mi> </mi><mi>v</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>y</mi><mi> </mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mi> </mi><mi>w</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>s</mi></math> ) close to:
solution:
Reynolds number

R=ρvDη

D =  Diameter of litre

Also rate of flow = volumetime=A v

Vt= π D24×v ⇒v=4VπD2t

∴    R= ρ Dη×4 Vπ D2 t

=4 ρ Vπ η D t

=4×103×15×10-3π×10-3×2×5×60 π×102

=10000π ≈5500

subject: Physics
q: A big drop is formed by coalescing 1000 small droplets of water. The ratio of surface energy of 1000 droplets to that of energy of big drop is $\frac{10}{x}$. The value of $x$ is __________
question: A big drop is formed by coalescing 1000 small droplets of water. The ratio of surface energy of 1000 droplets to that of energy of big drop is $\frac{10}{x}$. The value of $x$ is __________
solution: $\begin{aligned} 1000 \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 & =\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \\ 10 r & =R \\ R & =10 r\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} \frac{\text { S.E. of } 1000 \text { drops }}{\text { S.E. of Big drop }} & =\frac{1000\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2\right) \mathrm{T}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~T}} \\ & =\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{r}^2}{(10 \mathrm{r})^2}=10=\frac{10}{\mathrm{x}} \\ \therefore \quad x & =1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A small liquid drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is divided into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical liquid drops. If the surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, then the work done in the process will be :
question: A small liquid drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is divided into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical liquid drops. If the surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, then the work done in the process will be :
solution:
Let radius of the newly formed drops be r.

As total volume will remain constant, therefore

43πR3=27×43×πr3

⇒R3=27r3

⇒R=3r

⇒r=R3

⇒r2=R29

Now, work done =T×ΔA

=T27 4πr2-T4πR2=T27 4πR29-T4πR2=TπR212-4

=8πR2T

subject: Physics
q: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble is thrice the excess pressure inside a second soap bubble. The ratio between the volume of the first and the second bubble is:
question: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble is thrice the excess pressure inside a second soap bubble. The ratio between the volume of the first and the second bubble is:
solution: $\mathrm{P}_1-\mathrm{P}_0=\frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_1} \qquad \quad \quad \quad\mathrm{P}_2-\mathrm{P}_0=\frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_2}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}_1-\mathrm{P}_0=3\left(\mathrm{P}_2-\mathrm{P}_0\right) \\ & \frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_1}=3 \frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_2} \\ & \mathrm{r}_2=3 \mathrm{r}_1 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}_1^3}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}_2^3}=\frac{1}{27}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : When a capillary tube is dipped into a liquid, the liquid neither rises nor falls in the capillary. The contact angle may be $0^{\circ}$.
Statement II : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : When a capillary tube is dipped into a liquid, the liquid neither rises nor falls in the capillary. The contact angle may be $0^{\circ}$.
Statement II : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true and Statement II is false
solution:
Capillary rise
$\mathrm{h}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\rho \mathrm{gr}} \text {; }$
If $\theta=0^{\circ}$ then rise is non-zero
$\therefore$ Statement-1 is incorrect.

subject: Physics
q: When a long glass capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>015</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> is dipped in a liquid, the liquid rises to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> within it. If the contact angle between the liquid and glass to close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the surface tension of the liquid, in milliNewton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mtext>(liqued) </mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> (Given answer in closed integer)
question: When a long glass capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>015</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> is dipped in a liquid, the liquid rises to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> within it. If the contact angle between the liquid and glass to close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the surface tension of the liquid, in milliNewton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mtext>(liqued) </mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> (Given answer in closed integer)
solution:
Using the concept of rise due to surface tension,

 2Tr=hρg

T=rhρg2=15×10-5×15×10-2×900×102=101 milliNewton m-1

subject: Physics
q:
A water drop of radius 1μm falls in a situation where the effect of buoyant force is negligible. Co-efficient of viscosity of air is 1.8×10-5 N s m-2 and its density is negligible as compared to that of water 106 g m-3. Terminal velocity of the water drop is

(Take acceleration due to gravity =10 m s-2)
question:
A water drop of radius 1μm falls in a situation where the effect of buoyant force is negligible. Co-efficient of viscosity of air is 1.8×10-5 N s m-2 and its density is negligible as compared to that of water 106 g m-3. Terminal velocity of the water drop is

(Take acceleration due to gravity =10 m s-2)
solution:
Terminal velocity is given by  vT=2r2ρ-σg9η, where, r is radius of falling body, ρ is density of falling body and σ is density of fluid.

  ⇒vT=2×10-12×106×10-3×109×1.8×10-5

⇒vT=123.4×10-6 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
If 1000 droplets of water of surface tension 0.07 N m-1. having same radius 1 mm each, combine to from a single drop. In the process the released surface energy is-

Take π=227
question:
If 1000 droplets of water of surface tension 0.07 N m-1. having same radius 1 mm each, combine to from a single drop. In the process the released surface energy is-

Take π=227
solution:
Let the radius of bigger droplet is R, then by volume conservation:

43πR3=100043π13

⇒R=10 mm

Final potential energy:

Uf=10004πR2T=1000×4π10×10-32T

Initial potential energy:

Ui=4π×r2T=4π×10-32T

Therefore, change in potential energy,

∆E=T×1000×4π10-32-T×4π10×10-32

∆E=4×π×7×10-21000-100×10-6

∆E=7.92×10-4J

q: Consider a solid sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum density of a liquid in which it will float is:
question: Consider a solid sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum density of a liquid in which it will float is:
solution:
The density of any point inside the sphere is

ρ=ρ01-r2R2   0<r≤R

weight = buoyant force
mg = B

mg=ρLVg
∫ρ4πr2drg=ρL43πR3g
∫0Rρ01-r2R24πr2dr=ρL43πR3
∫0Rρ04πr2-r4R2dr=ρ04πr33-r55R20R=ρL43πR3
25ρ0=ρL

subject: Physics
q: A mercury drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn></math> equal size droplets. Surface tension of mercury is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The gain in surface energy is:
question: A mercury drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn></math> equal size droplets. Surface tension of mercury is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The gain in surface energy is:
solution:
As the volume of the mercury will remain same, therefore

43πR3=12543πr3⇒R=5r

Now, increase in surface energy

∆U=125×S×4πr2-S×4πR2

 =0.45×4π×10-3212525-1

=4×0.45×4π×10-6

=2.26×10-5J

subject: Physics
q: The top of a water tank is open to air and its water level is maintained. It is giving out <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.74</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> water per minute through a circular opening of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> radius in its wall. The depth of the centre of the opening from the level of water in the tank is close to:
question: The top of a water tank is open to air and its water level is maintained. It is giving out <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.74</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> water per minute through a circular opening of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> radius in its wall. The depth of the centre of the opening from the level of water in the tank is close to:
solution:
Volume of water flowing out=Av, where A is area of opening and v is speed of water from the opening.

Volume=πr2v

0.74  m3min=3.14×2×10-22×2×10×H m3s×60 smin

⇒H=0.7460×3.14×4×10-42×120

⇒H=4.8 m

Hence, the height of water in the tank was 4.8 m from the bottom of the tank.

subject: Physics
q: When a part of a straight capillary tube is placed vertically in a liquid, the liquid raises upto certain height $h$. If the inner radius of the capillary tube, density of the liquid and surface tension of the liquid decrease by $1 \%$ each, then the height of the liquid in the tube will change by $\%$.
question: When a part of a straight capillary tube is placed vertically in a liquid, the liquid raises upto certain height $h$. If the inner radius of the capillary tube, density of the liquid and surface tension of the liquid decrease by $1 \%$ each, then the height of the liquid in the tube will change by $\%$.
solution:
Capillary height formula: $h = \frac{2\sigma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$
When radius, density, and surface tension each decrease by 1%: $r' = 0.99r$, $\rho' = 0.99\rho$, $\sigma' = 0.99\sigma$
New height: $h' = \frac{2(0.99\sigma)\cos\theta}{(0.99\rho) g (0.99r)} = h \cdot \frac{0.99}{0.99 \times 0.99} = h \cdot \frac{1}{0.99} = h \cdot 1.0101...$
Percentage change: $(1.0101 - 1) \times 100 \approx +1.01\%$, which rounds to +1%.

subject: Physics
q: A large number of droplets, each of radius, $r$ coalesce to form a bigger drop of radius, $R$. An engineer designs a machine so that the energy released in this process is converted into the kinetic energy of the drop. Velocity of the drop is ( $T=$ surface tension, $\rho=$ density)
question: A large number of droplets, each of radius, $r$ coalesce to form a bigger drop of radius, $R$. An engineer designs a machine so that the energy released in this process is converted into the kinetic energy of the drop. Velocity of the drop is ( $T=$ surface tension, $\rho=$ density)
solution:
When small droplets coelesce to form a bigger drop, energy released in this process is given by,
$$
4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right]
$$
Where, $R=$ radius of big drop
$r=$ radius of small drop
$T=$ surface tension
According to question
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} m v^2=4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \rho\right] v^2=4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \\
\Rightarrow V^2 & =\frac{6 T}{\rho}\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \Rightarrow V=\left[\frac{6 T}{\rho}\left(\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A leak proof cylinder of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> made of a metal which has very low coefficient of expansion is floating vertically in water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> such that its height above the water surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the temperature of water is increased to <math><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the height of the cylinder above the water surface becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The density of water at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> relative to the density at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is close to:
question: A leak proof cylinder of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> made of a metal which has very low coefficient of expansion is floating vertically in water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> such that its height above the water surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the temperature of water is increased to <math><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the height of the cylinder above the water surface becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The density of water at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> relative to the density at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is close to:
solution:
According to Archemedes Principle, weight of the body is balanced by the upward thrust force due to buoyancy.

At 0°C, mg=A(100-20)ρ0°Cg, where A is area of cross-section of cylinder and ρ0°C is density of water at 0°C.
At 4°C, mg=A(100-21)ρ4°Cg, where ρ4°C is density of water at 4°C.
ρ4°Cρ0°C=8079=1.01

subject: Physics
q: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small identical drops of water. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> be the total surface energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small drops of water and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the surface energy of single big drop of water, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>________.
question: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small identical drops of water. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> be the total surface energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small drops of water and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the surface energy of single big drop of water, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>________.
solution:
Let radius of the small drop be r and the radius of the big drop be R. Then, total volume will remain constant, therefore

1000×ρ43πr3=43πR3ρ

⇒R=10r

Initial total surface energy, E1=1000×4πr2×S and 

E2=4π(10r)2 S

⇒E1E2=101Hence, x=10.

subject: Physics
q: Spherical balls of radius $R$ are falling in a viscous fluid of viscosity $\eta$ with a velocity $v$. The retarding viscous force acting on the spherical ball is
question: Spherical balls of radius $R$ are falling in a viscous fluid of viscosity $\eta$ with a velocity $v$. The retarding viscous force acting on the spherical ball is
options: directly proportional to $\mathrm{R}$ but inversely proportional to $\mathrm{v}$.
directly proportional to both radius $R$ and velocity $v$.
inversely proportional to both radius $\mathrm{R}$ and velocity $\mathrm{v}$.
inversely proportional to $\mathrm{R}$ but directly proportional to velocity $\mathrm{v}$.
solution: Retarding viscous force $=6 \pi \eta \mathrm{R} v$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
Statement II : The rise of a liquid in a capillary tube does not depend on the inner radius of the tube.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
Statement II : The rise of a liquid in a capillary tube does not depend on the inner radius of the tube.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
solution:
Statement I is correct as we know contact angle depends on cohesine and adhesive forces.
Statement II is incorrect because height of liquid is given by $h==\frac{2 T \cos \theta_C}{\rho g r}$ where $r$ is radius of Tube (assuming length of capillary is sufficient).

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: When you squeeze one end of a tube to get toothpaste out from the other end, Pascal’s principle is observed.

Reason R: A change in the pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of its container.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: When you squeeze one end of a tube to get toothpaste out from the other end, Pascal’s principle is observed.

Reason R: A change in the pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of its container.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is not correct but R is correct
A is correct but R is not correct
Both A and R is correct and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
Pascal's law states that the pressure in a closed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.

The formula for pressure is given by

P=FA

When a toothpaste is squeezed let the pressure transmitted by P. At the mouth of the toothpaste the pressure will also be equal to P due to Pascal's law. The pressure applied to the tube is transmitted equally throughout the toothpaste. When the pressure reaches the open end, it forces toothpaste out through the opening. Hence, both A&R is correct and R is correct explanation for  A.

subject: Physics
q: A water spray gun is attached to a hose of cross sectional area $30$ cm$^2$. The gun comprises of $10$ perforations each of cross sectional area $15$ mm$^2$. If the water flows in the hose with the speed of $50$ cm/s, calculate the speed at which the water flows out from each perforation. (Neglect any edge effects)
question: A water spray gun is attached to a hose of cross sectional area $30$ cm$^2$. The gun comprises of $10$ perforations each of cross sectional area $15$ mm$^2$. If the water flows in the hose with the speed of $50$ cm/s, calculate the speed at which the water flows out from each perforation. (Neglect any edge effects)
solution:
From the equation of continuity:

$A_1 v_1 = n A_2 v_2$

Here,
$A_1 = 30 \text{ cm}^2 = 30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$v_1 = 50 \text{ cm/s} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$
$n = 10$
$A_2 = 15 \text{ mm}^2 = 15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

Substituting the given values:

$(30 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.5 = 10 \times (15 \times 10^{-6}) \times v_2$

$15 \times 10^{-4} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times v_2$

$15 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times v_2$

$v_2 = \dfrac{15 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 10 \text{ m/s}$

Answer: $10$ m/s

subject: Physics
q: If two soap bubbles of different radii are connected by a tube,
question: If two soap bubbles of different radii are connected by a tube,
options: air flows from the bigger bubble to the smaller bubble till the sizes are interchanged.
air flows from bigger bubble to the smaller bubble till the sizes are interchanged
air flows from the smaller bubble to the bigger.
there is no flow of air.
solution:
The pressure inside the smaller bubble will be more $\left(P_i=P_0+\frac{4 T}{r}\right)$
Therefore, if the bubbles are connected by a tube, the air will flow from smaller bubble to the bigger.

subject: Physics
q: A small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> falls freely under gravity through a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> before entering a tank of water. If, after entering the water the velocity of ball does not change and it continue to fall with same constant velocity inside water, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, viscosity of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>‐</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ).
question: A small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> falls freely under gravity through a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> before entering a tank of water. If, after entering the water the velocity of ball does not change and it continue to fall with same constant velocity inside water, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, viscosity of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>‐</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ).
solution:
Using equation of motion, v2=u2+2as

v2=0+2-g-h

⇒v2=2gh

Initial velocity of ball is v=2gh=20h.

 If after entering the water this velocity does not change, then this value should be equal to the terminal velocity.

Then, 2gh=29r2ρ-σgη, here, ρ is density of ball, σ is density of water and η is viscosity.

⇒20h=290.1×10-32104-103101×10-5⇒20h=2910-810-1103×101×10-5

⇒20h=400

∴h=20 m
